source.title: Illuminating search spaces by mapping elites
source.venue: arXiv preprint (foundational QD paper; widely cited as equivalent to GECCO 2015 proceedings)

confidence_rationale: Foundational quality-diversity paper, extremely highly cited; introduces the behavioral descriptor / feature map concept that has become the standard approach to structural diversity preservation. Directly relevant to ARC-065. Core finding: maintaining a map of the best solution found per behavioral descriptor cell structurally prevents monostrategy by forcing the archive to retain high-quality solutions across the full behavioral space. Mapping fidelity moderate: MAP-Elites is a population/archive algorithm, not a single-policy algorithm; however the behavioral descriptor concept is directly applicable to REE's candidate evaluation.
failure_signatures: MAP-Elites requires a user-specified behavioral descriptor space. If the descriptor does not capture the behaviorally relevant dimensions of variation (e.g. specifying 'limb usage' for a robot but the relevant diversity axis is 'route taken'), the archive will be diverse on the wrong dimensions and monostrategy on the relevant ones.

Within each cell of the archive, MAP-Elites only retains the single best solution by fitness -- it does not maintain diversity within a cell. This means it enforces between-cell diversity but not within-cell diversity.
MAP-Elites scales poorly in very high-dimensional behavioral descriptor spaces (curse of dimensionality in the archive grid).

mapping.source_claim_statement: Maintaining a map of the best solution per cell in a user-specified behavioral descriptor space (the 'feature space') simultaneously optimises fitness and preserves diversity across the full descriptor space. Solutions compete locally within cells, not globally, so diverse high-performing solutions across the behavioral space are retained rather than eliminated by a single dominant strategy.
mapping.ree_translation: ARC-065 (behavioral diversity generation) and MECH-314 (curiosity/novelty bias) would benefit from defining a behavioral descriptor for REE's action selection. The natural descriptor for the V_s monostrategy problem is a trajectory-level measure: which region of the environment was visited, what reward mode was pursued (safety vs foraging), and at what step in the episode. If REE's CEM maintains a soft archive over these descriptors -- preferring candidates that cover under-represented behavioral regions -- this is a local implementation of MAP-Elites within the candidate selection step. The behavioral descriptor is the key design decision: it must capture route-mode diversity, not just action diversity.
mapping.mapping_caveat: MAP-Elites is a population algorithm (offline optimization across many solutions). REE's CEM is a real-time per-step candidate generator. A direct port would require maintaining a per-episode behavioral archive that persists between steps and biases CEM sampling toward under-represented cells. This is computationally feasible but architecturally novel. The deeper challenge is that the behavioral descriptor must be specified in advance (or learned), and REE does not currently have a route-mode descriptor.
mapping.source_context: Three domains: modular neural networks, simulated soft robots, real soft robots. Behavioral descriptors are domain-specific (e.g. limb proportions for robots). Evolutionary algorithm (mutation only, no crossover in original version).

confidence_components.notes: Foundational paper; extremely high impact in QD literature. Mapping fidelity moderate because MAP-Elites is a population/offline algorithm while REE needs within-episode diversity. The behavioral descriptor insight transfers cleanly; the archive mechanism requires significant adaptation.
